title: Checkpoint Modernization Act
plain_summary: The Checkpoint Modernization Act tells U.S. Customs and Border Protection to focus on fixing and improving Border Patrol checkpoints along the southern border with Mexico. The goal is to make these checkpoints safer for officers, better at catching people who smuggle things illegally, and less likely to cause traffic jams. Congress will give at least 150 million dollars every year for four years starting in 2025 to pay for these improvements. Some of this money will come from taking away unused environmental grant money that was never spent.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Checkpoint Modernization Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Renovation of U.S. Border Patrol Checkpoints
sections.text: The head of U.S. Customs and Border Protection must focus on fixing up Border Patrol checkpoints, but only if Congress gives them money to do it. They must put these improvement projects first if they directly make three things better. First, they must make Border Patrol officers safer and improve their working conditions. Second, they must help Border Patrol agents do a better job finding and stopping people who smuggle other people across the border and illegal items like drugs. Third, they must reduce traffic jams and make roads safer for everyone because more vehicles are crossing the southern border between the United States and Mexico. The head of U.S. Customs and Border Protection must send a report to two committees in Congress. One committee is the House Committee on Homeland Security. The other committee is the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs. The first report is due no later than one hundred eighty days after this law starts. After that, they must send a report every year. Each report must explain how much the checkpoint improvements will cost, how the work is going, why the improvements are needed, and any other important information about fixing up these Border Patrol checkpoints. Congress allows the head of U.S. Customs and Border Protection to spend at least one hundred fifty million dollars each year from twenty twenty five through twenty twenty eight to do this work. All money that the Environmental Protection Agency has not yet spent on environmental and climate justice block grants under section one hundred thirty eight of the Clean Air Act is permanently taken away and cannot be used.
